old_claim.note: Public Key Infrastructure provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: Hardware security modules protect and process cryptographic keys for many applications; PKI is one application domain rather than a prerequisite of the module class.

source_shape.source_locator: Validated-module certificate 4765, module description and application list: the HSM performs key management, encryption, and signing for PKI, TLS, identity, code signing, and application encryption.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: NIST's validation record documents an HSM as a general cryptographic processor serving PKI alongside several non-PKI application classes.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Listing PKI as one consumer among independent encryption, signing, TLS, and identity uses establishes that certificate infrastructure consumes HSM capabilities rather than constituting the hardware category.

ontology_before: The graph treated one major use of secure cryptographic hardware as a cause of the hardware module itself.
ontology_after: HSMs remain general key-protection and cryptographic-processing devices that may support PKI or unrelated cryptographic applications.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed and locked by graph invariant 2026-07-16-pki-independent-identity-and-primitive-boundaries: hardware_security_modules no longer directly depends on public_key_infrastructure.
would_reject_if: Authoritative module definitions restrict HSMs to X.509 certificate-authority infrastructure and exclude independent encryption or signing uses.
short_reason: NIST validates HSMs for several cryptographic application classes, making PKI a consumer rather than a prerequisite.
